retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Primary Dentition -- Structure and Function -- Mandibular Primary Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: Second Molars – The mandibular second molars(tooth T, K) morphologically resemble the first mandibular molar of the permanent teeth. Its buccal surface is divided into the mesiobuccal, distobuccal, and distal cusps. The lingual cusps include the distolingual and the mesiolingual cusps. Numerous differences exist between the primary and permanent teeth, for example: Primary teeth are smaller Crowding is uncommon in primary teeth due to adequate space Primary teeth are whiter Pulp chambers of primary teeth are larger The enamel and dentin of primary teeth are thinner The area of contact of the primary molars are flat and broad The roots of the primary molar teeth are narrow and slender Pulp size relative to the crown is larger in primary teeth

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Primary Dentition -- Structure and Function -- Maxillary Primary Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: First Molars – The maxillary first molars (tooth B, I) morphologically resemble both a molar and a premolar. Its occlusal surface consists of the mesiobuccal, distolingual, mesiolingual, and distobuccal cusps. The largest cusp is the mesiolingual cusp, and it also represents the sharpest cusp. The distolingual cusp is small and rounded in morphology. The buccal surface is smooth, and the three roots are long and widely spread. Of note, the primary 1st molar has a less pronounced mesiobuccal cusp when compared to the permanent 1st molar.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Primary Dentition -- Structure and Function -- Maxillary Primary Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: Molars – The molars are the largest of the primary teeth and provide significant function in mastication. The primary teeth molars have the same number of roots as the permanent molars. The crowns of the primary molars are more bulbous in morphology, and the roots are more divergent than those of permanent molars.  The buccal, lingual, mesial, and distal surfaces of the maxillary molars are all convex. The occlusal surface is rectangular.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Primary Dentition -- Structure and Function -- Mandibular Primary Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: The dental morphology of the mandibular teeth mirrors that of the maxillary teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Primary Dentition -- Structure and Function -- Maxillary Primary Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: Second Molars – Morphologically, the primary maxillary second molars (tooth A, J) resemble the permanent maxillary first molar. Rhomboidal in shape, the maxillary second molar has four cusps, two on both the buccal and lingual aspects. A fifth cusp can be present and is called the tubercle of Carabelli. Two well-defined cusps can be seen on the buccal view, and the lingual surface has three cusps: the mesiolingual, distolingual, and the cusp of Carabelli.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Primary Dentition -- Structure and Function -- Mandibular Primary Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: First Molars – The mandibular first molars (tooth S, L) have four cusps: two buccal and two lingual. These include the distobuccal, distolingual, mesiolingual, and mesiobuccal cusps. The occlusal surface is narrow secondary to mesiobuccal and mesiolingual cusp convergence. There are three pits.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Occlusal Contact Relations And Mandibular Movements -- Structure and Function -- Maxillary and Mandibular Dental Arch
retrieved_docs.content: The permanent, succedaneous teeth in adults consist of 16 teeth in the maxilla and 16 teeth in the mandible. The universal numbering system for permanent teeth assigns numbers to each tooth from 1 to 32. Tooth #1 is the right maxillary third molar followed sequentially by numbers 2-15 across the maxilla until reaching the left maxillary third molar, tooth #16. Numbering continues down to the mandible, with tooth #17 being the left mandibular third molar followed by 18-31 proceeding sequentially across the mandible until reaching the right mandibular third molar, which is tooth #32. [7] [8] There are alternative numbering systems.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Permanent Dentition -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Humans have two sets of teeth during their lifetime: the initial deciduous (primary) teeth and the successive permanent (secondary) teeth. [1] There are typically 20 deciduous teeth divided evenly across the maxilla and mandible. The deciduous teeth eventually exfoliate and are replaced by 32 permanent teeth: 16 in the maxilla and 16 in the mandible. Permanent teeth are classified as incisors, canines, premolars, and molars, while primary teeth do not include premolars.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Primary Dentition -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The primary dentition should not be confused with the permanent dentition, composed of 32 teeth with 16 teeth in each arch. Each of the primary molars is replaced by permanent, premolar teeth. There are three permanent molars in each quadrant that are not succeeded by primary teeth and may be referred to as non-succedaneous teeth. The arrival of these teeth, 12 in total, gives permanent dentition 32 teeth compared to the 20 teeth of primary dentition.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Primary Dentition -- Structure and Function -- Mandibular Primary Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: Incisors - The incisors of the mandible are smaller than the incisors of the maxillary teeth and have one root that contains a single root canal. The central incisors constitute teeth P and O. The lateral incisors constitute teeth Q and N. The root of the incisors are slightly flattened. The crown is trapezoidal in the labial view and contains three tubercled incisal edges. Canines – The canines constitute teeth R and M. Their crown is trapezoidal with a single labial cusp. A single root (the longest of the teeth) contains a single root canal. Mandibular molars : The buccal, lingual, mesial, and distal surfaces of the mandibular molars are all convex. The occlusal surface is rectangular.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Tooth impaction may affect anyone, with incidence rates varying from 5.6% to 18.8%, and it tends to be more common in female individuals. The frequency of tooth impaction follows a pattern opposite to the typical chronological eruption sequence. Teeth that erupt later are more likely to be impacted due to insufficient arch space. The mandibular 3rd molar is the most commonly impacted tooth, followed by the maxillary 3rd molar, maxillary canine, mandibular premolars, maxillary premolars, and 2nd molar. The teeth with the lowest impaction rates are the mandibular incisors, 1st molars, and primary teeth. Impaction of these teeth is relatively rare and is typically associated with specific conditions, such as retained deciduous teeth or abnormalities like an odontoma.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Primary Dentition -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: When divided on the mid-line sagittal plane, these teeth are organized into the right and left halves. Incisors and canines can be classified further as "anterior teeth" and molars and premolars as "posterior teeth." The primary teeth erupt during the first year and into the third years of life and are then exfoliated(shed) between the ages of 6 and 12. Since primary teeth are shed, as some trees shed their leaves, they are also referred to as deciduous teeth, which are eventually replaced by the adult permanent dentition. [2] Primary teeth are labeled in the following classification system and are designated in a clockwise direction: A: Right maxillary 2nd molar B: Right maxillary 1st molar C: Right maxillary canine D: Right maxillary lateral incisor E: Right maxillary central incisor F: Left maxillary central incisor G: Left maxillary lateral incisor H: Left maxillary canine I: Left maxillary 1st molar J: Left maxillary 2nd molar T: Right mandibular 2nd molar

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Finally, the mandibular third molars typically have two shorter, often parallel, mesial, and distal roots with a more pronounced taper than the first or second mandibular molars. [8] Like the maxillary third molar, the mandibular third molar can have significant morphological variation. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Teeth -- Structure and Function -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: Teeth are calcified structures found in the oral cavity embedded to the upper jaw (maxilla) and lower jaw (mandible). Human teeth are heterodont and characterized by four tooth classes: incisors, canines, premolars, and molars. Human teeth are also diphyodont because there are two generations of teeth during a lifespan: 20 deciduous (primary) teeth and 32 permanent teeth. The primary dentition consists of the two types of incisors - central and lateral, canines, and two types of molars - first and second. The primary incisors, canines, and molars get replaced by the successional permanent incisors, canines, and premolars. Permanent dentition also consists of the additional teeth that are three types of molars - first, second, and third. The process fo identifying teeth uses the Universal National System. In the maxilla, permanent adult teeth are numbered 1 through 16 from right to left, and primary teeth are labeled with letters A through J from right to left. In the mandible, permanent adult teeth are numbered 17 through 32 from left to right, and primary teeth are labeled with letters K through T from left to right. [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Primary Dentition -- Structure and Function -- Maxillary Primary Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: Canines – The primary teeth canines are also morphologically similar to permanent teeth canines.  The canines (tooth C, H) have a trapezoidal crown with one labial cusp. The labial surface is convex, and the palatal surface is concave. The canines have a single long root and a single root canal.  The canine roots are the longest root of all teeth. Furthermore, the upper canines have longer roots when compared to the mandibular canines. The canines serve to support the incisors. The canines have a characteristic pointed incisal edge and a large cone-shaped crown.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Permanent Dentition -- Physiologic Variants
retrieved_docs.content: Tooth agenesis may involve the primary or permanent dentition and is a developmental anomaly where at least one tooth is absent. It can be further classified as hypodontia, involving 1 to 5 absent teeth, and oligodontia involving six or more missing teeth. When excluding third molars (wisdom teeth), tooth agenesis has a prevalence between 3% and 10% in the permanent dentition. Third molars are the most commonly congenitally missing permanent teeth, with a reported prevalence of 23%. This is followed by mandibular second premolars, maxillary lateral incisors, and, less frequently, the maxillary second premolars. [37]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: The extraction of a single tooth is a direct dental procedure performed on the zuby (teeth), involving the removal of one tooth from the oral cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is classified as a dental procedure, indicating that it has a relationship with the broader category of dental procedures. This connection underscores that the act of extracting a single tooth is a specific type of dental intervention within the overall field of dental procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are performed directly on zuby mléčné (the primary or “milk” teeth), indicating that therapeutic dental interventions often target the primary dentition. Likewise, disorders of teeth and jaw are located in zuby mléčné, showing that many oral pathologies also affect these early‑life teeth. Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 243
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of teeth and jaw are classified under the broader parent category zuby and are specifically associated with the anatomical location of zuby mléčné, the primary (milk) teeth.

retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 265

This integrated framework supports accurate diagnosis, prognostication, and site‑specific therapeutic planning for patients with diverse malignant neoplasms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 0 -- Community 19
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a dental procedure that directly targets teeth (zuby) and is recognized as part of the broader set of dental procedures. Dental procedures themselves are also performed directly on deciduous teeth (zuby mléčné). Disorders of teeth and jaw are categorized under the parent concept of teeth (zuby) and are specifically located in the deciduous dentition (zuby mléčné). Consequently, both the extraction of a single tooth and other dental procedures may be applied to permanent and baby teeth, while disorders of the teeth and jaw primarily affect the baby teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: Melanomas of skin are malignant neoplasms that arise from melanocytes in the cutaneous tissue and represent a specific form of skin cancer. سرطان الشفة الخبيث, which translates to malignant lip cancer, is a distinct malignant tumor that originates in the lip and is classified as an oral cavity cancer. Although both conditions are cancers, they affect different anatomical sites and are not subtypes of one another; each requires separate diagnostic and therapeutic approaches tailored to its location and biological behavior. Understanding the differences between melanomas of skin and سرطان الشفة الخبيث is important for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment planning, and effective patient care.

Recognizing sodium monofluorophosphate as a fluoride source guides oral‑health preventive strategies, while awareness of the SMQ classification aids clinicians in systematic identification and treatment of functional GI disturbances, underscoring the importance of these entities for patient care and product design.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2709
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is identified as a specific form of malignant tumor in the sublingual gland, as indicated by its inverse_isa relationship to ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان. Both Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland and the broader category Neoplasm, uncertain whether benign or malignant share the relationship termed النقيلة, suggesting a common classification or characteristic within neoplastic disease. Recognizing that the metastatic malignant neoplasm to the sublingual gland represents a subset of malignant sublingual gland tumors underscores its aggressive potential and the need for thorough evaluation, while the ambiguous neoplasm category highlights the importance of histopathological assessment to determine benign versus malignant status. The linking term النقيلة therefore serves as a unifying descriptor that aids clinicians in diagnosing, categorizing, and managing these related neoplastic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2573
retrieved_docs.content: Metastasis to head and neck lymph node is classified as a secondary malignancy, indicating that the spread of cancer to these regional lymph nodes represents a secondary tumor rather than a primary disease. Recognizing this mapping is important for accurate staging, prognosis assessment, and the selection of appropriate therapeutic strategies in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2387
retrieved_docs.content: The phrase “Due to” serves as a causal indicator and is associated with specific injury outcomes, including loss of teeth resulting from an accident (“verlies; tanden door ongeval”) as well as severe trauma such as crushing injuries or internal injuries. Recognizing this wording helps clinicians link the underlying cause to these particular clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 204
retrieved_docs.content: pancreatic cancer. Metastatic carcinoma to a lymph node of the head and neck and metastatic squamous cell carcinoma of lymph nodes of the head and neck are both characterized as cancerous tumors; the latter is an inverse ISA of squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck (سرطان الخلايا الحرشفية في الرأس والعنق) and carries the specific morphology of metastatic squamous cell carcinoma (سرطانة الخلايا الحرشفية النقيلية). The non‑hematologic malignant diseases group (الأمراض غير الدموية الخبيثة (SMQ)) functions as a container for secondary malignant tumors in other specified sites, malignant tumors of the upper jaw (ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي), and malignant tumor of the sublingual gland, and it is itself a member of the broader metastatic cancer category (السرطان النقيلي). This SMQ group is also listed as a member of both pancreatic cancer and melanomas of the skin, indicating that these disease families belong to the non‑hematologic malignant disease spectrum.
